It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 124 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

Looking at the image:
- Bordered: purple axis-aligned (with white underline on right legend item)
- Below: purple square on top, white square on bottom
The legend items have changed! Now the right item (purple-above-white) has the white underline. This means clicking the purple legend item changed the target from white-above-purple to purple-above-white.
The rectangles below are purple on top and white on bottom. This is purple-above-white, which matches the target!
Wait, but the level hasn't completed. Let me check the segmentation to see the exact state.
Actually, looking at the image, the rectangles below are purple on top and white on bottom. This is exactly the target (purple-above-white). But the level hasn't completed.
What if the goal is not just to have the rectangles match the target, but also to have the bordered rectangle be a specific color and rotation? Currently, the bordered is purple axis-aligned.
What if the goal is to have the bordered be purple axis-aligned and the rectangles be purple-above-white (50-50)? Currently, the bordered is purple axis-aligned, and the rectangles are purple-above-white. But the level hasn't completed.
Let me check the segmentation to see if the rectangles are 50-50 or not.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current segmentation
after_seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Main area nodes:")
for n in after_seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] >= 10 and n['pixels'] < 200:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Also check legend items
print("\nLegend area nodes:")
for n in after_seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] < 10:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- id=14: white, 50 pixels
The rectangles are exactly 50-50 purple-white! And the target is purple-above-white (right item has underline).

What else is needed? Let me look at the bordered rectangle. It's purple axis-aligned (id=12, purple, 84 pixels).
What if the goal is to have the bordered rectangle match the target legend item? The target is purple-above-white, which is two rectangles. The bordered is a single rectangle. So this doesn't make sense.
What if the goal is to have the bordered be a specific color and rotation, and the rectangles below be 50-50 matching the target? Currently, the bordered is purple axis-aligned, and the rectangles are 50-50 purple-above-white. But the level hasn't completed.
Let me try rotating the bordered to diamond and checking if the level completes.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the legend items. The left item is white-above-purple, and the right item is purple-above-white. The white underline on the right item indicates it's the target.
What if the goal is to have the bordered rectangle be purple diamond and the rectangles below be 50-50 purple-above-white? Currently, the bordered is purple axis-aligned. Let me try rotating to diamond.

sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Target: purple-above-white (right item has underline)
# Rectangles match target (50-50)
# Try: rotate bordered to diamond and check if level completes
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
